240	curve:
241	
242	```{r}
243	sample_data(dfm_rarefied)$metacommunity <- "all"
244	```
245	
246	```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction for the entire metacommunity (q = 1)"}
247	hill_acc_pq(
248	  dfm_rarefied,
249	  q = 1,
250	  type = "individual",
251	  merge_sample_by = "metacommunity",
252	  n_simulations = 10
253	) +
254	  no_legend() +
255	  ggtitle("Metacommunity rarefaction (q = 1)")
256	```
257	
258	### Per-sample rarefaction
259	
260	Examining individual samples reveals heterogeneity in diversity and sampling
261	completeness. Samples whose curve has not plateaued may harbour undetected
262	taxa.
263	
264	```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction for 5 random samples at different Hill orders"}
265	random_samples <- sample(sample_names(dfm_rarefied), 5)
266	dfm_5 <- prune_samples(random_samples, dfm_rarefied)
267	
268	p_q0 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 0, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
269	  ggtitle("q = 0 (richness)")
270	p_q1 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 1, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
271	  ggtitle("q = 1 (Shannon)")
272	p_q2 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 2, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
273	  ggtitle("q = 2 (Simpson)")
274	
275	(p_q0 + p_q1 + p_q2) & no_legend()
276	```
277	
278	At q = 0, curves rarely plateau because rare OTUs (many of which may be
279	artefacts) keep accumulating with additional sequences. At q = 1 and q = 2,
280	curves saturate faster because dominant OTUs are detected early. This
281	illustrates why q = {1, 2} provides more reliable sampling completeness
282	assessments [@Calderon2019].
283	
284	### Rarefaction by habitat
285	
286	Merging samples by the `Height` variable aggregates sequences within each
287	habitat, enabling comparison at the habitat scale:
288	
289	```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction by Height (q = 1)"}
290	hill_acc_pq(
291	  dfm_rarefied,
292	  q = 1,
293	  type = "individual",
294	  merge_sample_by = "Height",
295	  n_simulations = 0
296	) +
297	  ggtitle("Rarefaction by Height (q = 1)")
298	```
299	
300	## Sample-based accumulation (abundance-based)
301	
302	Sample-based accumulation plots Hill diversity as a function of the number
303	of **samples** added. It answers: *does spatial (or temporal) sampling
304	capture the full diversity?* It reflects turnover across samples
305	(beta-diversity), not sequencing depth within samples.
306	
307	`hill_acc_pq()` with `type = "sample"` pools samples in random order across
308	`n_permutations` replicates and computes Hill diversity of the pooled
309	community at each step. The shaded band shows the confidence envelope.
310	
311	Note: even though this approach aggregates samples, the pooled community
312	is still evaluated using **sequence counts** — it remains abundance-based.
313	Compare with the incidence-based approach in the next section.
314	
315	```{r, fig.cap="Sample-based accumulation for the entire dataset (q = 1, 50 permutations)"}
316	hill_acc_pq(
317	  dfm_rarefied,
318	  q = 1,
319	  type = "sample",
320	  n_permutations = 50
321	) +
322	  ggtitle("Sample-based accumulation (q = 1)")
323	```
324	
325	By splitting by habitat, one can assess whether diversity saturates within
326	each level and how much each level contributes to the total:
327	
328	```{r, fig.cap="Sample-based accumulation by Height (q = 1)"}
329	hill_acc_pq(
330	  dfm_rarefied,
331	  q = 1,
332	  type = "sample",
333	  merge_sample_by = "Height",
334	  n_permutations = 50
335	) +
336	  ggtitle("Sample-based accumulation by Height (q = 1)")
337	```
338	
339	## Incidence-based approach
340	
341	When sequence counts are not trusted as proxies for abundance — for instance
342	due to primer binding biases or cross-contamination — an incidence-based
343	approach uses only presence/absence of OTUs across samples.
344	`as_binary_otu_table()` converts the OTU table to 0/1 before analysis.
345	
346	Because incidence-based Hill numbers measure the effective number of OTUs
347	weighted by the fraction of samples they occur in, they are only meaningful
348	at the level of the entire system and are most naturally used with
349	`type = "sample"`.
350	
351	```{r}
352	data(data_fungi)
353	dfm_binary <- as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi)
354	```
355	
356	```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based sample accumulation (q = 1)"}
357	hill_acc_pq(
358	  dfm_binary,
359	  q = 1,
360	  type = "sample",
361	  n_permutations = 50
362	) +
363	  ggtitle("Incidence-based sample accumulation (q = 1)")
364	```
365	
366	```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based sample accumulation by Height (q = 1)"}
367	data_fungi_woNA4Height <- subset_samples_pq(
368	  data_fungi,
369	  !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height)
370	)
371	dfm_binary_height <- as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi_woNA4Height)
372	
373	hill_acc_pq(
374	  dfm_binary_height,
375	  q = 1,
376	  type = "sample",
377	  merge_sample_by = "Height",
378	  n_permutations = 50
379	) +
380	  ggtitle("Incidence-based sample accumulation by Height (q = 1)")
381	```
382	
383	Comparing the abundance-based and incidence-based accumulation curves reveals
384	how much the diversity ordering among habitats depends on quantitative
385	sequence information vs. simple detection patterns.
386	
387	## Diversity profiles
388	
389	Diversity profiles show how the Hill diversity index varies continuously
390	across orders *q*, for a fixed dataset. `profile_hill_pq()` wraps
391	`divent::profile_hill()`.
392	
393	A steeply declining profile indicates an uneven community dominated by a few
394	OTUs. A flat profile indicates high evenness. The shape of the profile is
395	itself informative: two communities with identical diversity at q = 1 may
396	have very different profiles, revealing different abundance distributions.
397	
398	### Abundance-based profiles
399

file.content:
curve:

```{r}
sample_data(dfm_rarefied)$metacommunity <- "all"
```

```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction for the entire metacommunity (q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_rarefied,
  q = 1,
  type = "individual",
  merge_sample_by = "metacommunity",
  n_simulations = 10
) +
  no_legend() +
  ggtitle("Metacommunity rarefaction (q = 1)")
```

### Per-sample rarefaction

Examining individual samples reveals heterogeneity in diversity and sampling
completeness. Samples whose curve has not plateaued may harbour undetected
taxa.

```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction for 5 random samples at different Hill orders"}
random_samples <- sample(sample_names(dfm_rarefied), 5)
dfm_5 <- prune_samples(random_samples, dfm_rarefied)

p_q0 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 0, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
  ggtitle("q = 0 (richness)")
p_q1 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 1, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
  ggtitle("q = 1 (Shannon)")
p_q2 <- hill_acc_pq(dfm_5, q = 2, type = "individual", n_simulations = 0) +
  ggtitle("q = 2 (Simpson)")

(p_q0 + p_q1 + p_q2) & no_legend()
```

At q = 0, curves rarely plateau because rare OTUs (many of which may be
artefacts) keep accumulating with additional sequences. At q = 1 and q = 2,
curves saturate faster because dominant OTUs are detected early. This
illustrates why q = {1, 2} provides more reliable sampling completeness
assessments [@Calderon2019].

### Rarefaction by habitat

Merging samples by the `Height` variable aggregates sequences within each
habitat, enabling comparison at the habitat scale:

```{r, fig.cap="Individual-based rarefaction by Height (q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_rarefied,
  q = 1,
  type = "individual",
  merge_sample_by = "Height",
  n_simulations = 0
) +
  ggtitle("Rarefaction by Height (q = 1)")
```

## Sample-based accumulation (abundance-based)

Sample-based accumulation plots Hill diversity as a function of the number
of **samples** added. It answers: *does spatial (or temporal) sampling
capture the full diversity?* It reflects turnover across samples
(beta-diversity), not sequencing depth within samples.

`hill_acc_pq()` with `type = "sample"` pools samples in random order across
`n_permutations` replicates and computes Hill diversity of the pooled
community at each step. The shaded band shows the confidence envelope.

Note: even though this approach aggregates samples, the pooled community
is still evaluated using **sequence counts** — it remains abundance-based.
Compare with the incidence-based approach in the next section.

```{r, fig.cap="Sample-based accumulation for the entire dataset (q = 1, 50 permutations)"}
hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_rarefied,
  q = 1,
  type = "sample",
  n_permutations = 50
) +
  ggtitle("Sample-based accumulation (q = 1)")
```

By splitting by habitat, one can assess whether diversity saturates within
each level and how much each level contributes to the total:

```{r, fig.cap="Sample-based accumulation by Height (q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_rarefied,
  q = 1,
  type = "sample",
  merge_sample_by = "Height",
  n_permutations = 50
) +
  ggtitle("Sample-based accumulation by Height (q = 1)")
```

## Incidence-based approach

When sequence counts are not trusted as proxies for abundance — for instance
due to primer binding biases or cross-contamination — an incidence-based
approach uses only presence/absence of OTUs across samples.
`as_binary_otu_table()` converts the OTU table to 0/1 before analysis.

Because incidence-based Hill numbers measure the effective number of OTUs
weighted by the fraction of samples they occur in, they are only meaningful
at the level of the entire system and are most naturally used with
`type = "sample"`.

```{r}
data(data_fungi)
dfm_binary <- as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi)
```

```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based sample accumulation (q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_binary,
  q = 1,
  type = "sample",
  n_permutations = 50
) +
  ggtitle("Incidence-based sample accumulation (q = 1)")
```

```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based sample accumulation by Height (q = 1)"}
data_fungi_woNA4Height <- subset_samples_pq(
  data_fungi,
  !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height)
)
dfm_binary_height <- as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi_woNA4Height)

hill_acc_pq(
  dfm_binary_height,
  q = 1,
  type = "sample",
  merge_sample_by = "Height",
  n_permutations = 50
) +
  ggtitle("Incidence-based sample accumulation by Height (q = 1)")
```

Comparing the abundance-based and incidence-based accumulation curves reveals
how much the diversity ordering among habitats depends on quantitative
sequence information vs. simple detection patterns.

## Diversity profiles

Diversity profiles show how the Hill diversity index varies continuously
across orders *q*, for a fixed dataset. `profile_hill_pq()` wraps
`divent::profile_hill()`.

A steeply declining profile indicates an uneven community dominated by a few
OTUs. A flat profile indicates high evenness. The shape of the profile is
itself informative: two communities with identical diversity at q = 1 may
have very different profiles, revealing different abundance distributions.

### Abundance-based profiles

content.content:
395	itself informative: two communities with identical diversity at q = 1 may
396	have very different profiles, revealing different abundance distributions.
397	
398	### Abundance-based profiles
399	
400	```{r, fig.cap="Abundance-based Hill diversity profiles per sample"}
401	profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) + no_legend()
402	```
403	
404	```{r, fig.cap="Abundance-based Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
405	profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations = 10)
406	```
407	
408	### Incidence-based profiles
409	
410	Applying `profile_hill_pq()` to a binary OTU table yields incidence-based
411	diversity profiles, where each OTU is weighted by the proportion of samples
412	it occurs in rather than its sequence count:
413	
414	```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
415	profile_hill_pq(
416	  as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi_woNA4Height),
417	  merge_sample_by = "Height",
418	  n_simulations = 10
419	)
420	```
421	
422	Contrasting the abundance-based and incidence-based profiles highlights which
423	diversity patterns are robust to the quantitative interpretation of sequence
424	counts and which depend on it.
425	
426	## Beyond size-based rarefaction: coverage-based approaches
427	
428	The approaches above standardise by **sample size** (number of sequences or
429	number of samples). An emerging alternative standardises by **sample
430	coverage** — the estimated fraction of the community already detected in a
431	sample [@Chao2020]. This matters because the same sequencing depth can yield
432	very different coverages depending on community evenness: a highly uneven
433	community (a few dominant OTUs) achieves high coverage quickly, while an
434	even one requires far more sequences.
435	
436	Importantly, when the treatment or environment of interest also affects
437	community evenness (e.g. high land-use intensity simplifies communities),
438	size-based rarefaction conflates diversity loss with coverage gain.
439	@Kortmann2025 demonstrated this directly: coverage-standardised Hill numbers
440	revealed a 27–44% stronger diversity decline along a land-use gradient in
441	insects than size-based rarefaction suggested. The theoretical foundation
442	(sample completeness profiles parameterised by *q*) is formalised in
443	@Chao2020.
444	
445	Coverage-based rarefaction is not yet implemented in `hill_acc_pq()`, but is
446	available via the [iNEXT.4steps](https://cran.r-project.org/package=iNEXT.4steps)
447	package, which accepts both abundance and incidence data and produces
448	rarefaction/extrapolation curves standardised by coverage for any Hill
449	order *q* [@Chao2020]. It is worth running alongside the curves produced
450	here whenever there is reason to suspect that sampling coverage varies across
451	the samples being compared.
452	
453	## Summary
454

itself informative: two communities with identical diversity at q = 1 may
have very different profiles, revealing different abundance distributions.

### Abundance-based profiles

```{r, fig.cap="Abundance-based Hill diversity profiles per sample"}
profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Abundance-based Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations = 10)
```

### Incidence-based profiles

Applying `profile_hill_pq()` to a binary OTU table yields incidence-based
diversity profiles, where each OTU is weighted by the proportion of samples
it occurs in rather than its sequence count:

```{r, fig.cap="Incidence-based Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
profile_hill_pq(
  as_binary_otu_table(data_fungi_woNA4Height),
  merge_sample_by = "Height",
  n_simulations = 10
)
```

Contrasting the abundance-based and incidence-based profiles highlights which
diversity patterns are robust to the quantitative interpretation of sequence
counts and which depend on it.

## Beyond size-based rarefaction: coverage-based approaches

The approaches above standardise by **sample size** (number of sequences or
number of samples). An emerging alternative standardises by **sample
coverage** — the estimated fraction of the community already detected in a
sample [@Chao2020]. This matters because the same sequencing depth can yield
very different coverages depending on community evenness: a highly uneven
community (a few dominant OTUs) achieves high coverage quickly, while an
even one requires far more sequences.

Importantly, when the treatment or environment of interest also affects
community evenness (e.g. high land-use intensity simplifies communities),
size-based rarefaction conflates diversity loss with coverage gain.
@Kortmann2025 demonstrated this directly: coverage-standardised Hill numbers
revealed a 27–44% stronger diversity decline along a land-use gradient in
insects than size-based rarefaction suggested. The theoretical foundation
(sample completeness profiles parameterised by *q*) is formalised in
@Chao2020.

Coverage-based rarefaction is not yet implemented in `hill_acc_pq()`, but is
available via the [iNEXT.4steps](https://cran.r-project.org/package=iNEXT.4steps)
package, which accepts both abundance and incidence data and produces
rarefaction/extrapolation curves standardised by coverage for any Hill
order *q* [@Chao2020]. It is worth running alongside the curves produced
here whenever there is reason to suspect that sampling coverage varies across
the samples being compared.

## Summary

7	  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
8	editor_options: 
9	  chunk_output_type: console
10	---
11	
12	```{r, include = FALSE}
13	knitr::opts_chunk$set(
14	  collapse = TRUE,
15	  comment = "#>",
16	  message = FALSE
17	)
18	```
19	
20	```{r setup, message=FALSE}
21	library(MiscMetabar)
22	data(data_fungi)
23	```
24	
25	### Alpha diversity analysis
26	
27	#### Hill number
28	
29	Numerous metrics of diversity exist. 
30	Hill numbers [^Hill73] is a kind of general framework for alpha diversity index. 
31	
32	[^Hill73]: Hill MO. 1973. Diversity and evenness: a unifying notation and its consequences. Ecology 54, 427-473.
33	
34	
35	#### Diversity profiles
36	
37	`profile_hill_pq()` plots the Hill diversity profile (diversity as a function of the order *q*) for each sample, or for groups of samples merged via `merge_sample_by`.
38	
39	```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles per sample (data_fungi_mini)"}
40	data(data_fungi_mini)
41	dfm_rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi_mini, rngseed = 1, sample.size=200)
42	p <-profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) 
43	
44	p + no_legend()
45	```
46	
47	```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
48	profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height")
49	```
50	
51	#### Rarefaction curves
52	
53	`hill_acc_pq()` plots Hill diversity accumulation (rarefaction) curves — one per sample or per merged group — showing how estimated diversity grows with sequencing depth.
54	
55	```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves per sample (Hill order q = 1)"}
56	hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations=20) + no_legend()
57	```
58	
59	```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves merged by Height (Hill order q = 0)"}
60	hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations=20)
61	```
62	
63	#### Test for difference in diversity (hill number)
64	
65	One way to keep into account for difference in the number of sequences per samples is to use a Tukey test on a linear model with the square roots of the number of sequence as the first explanatory variable of the linear model [^Balint15].
66	
67	[^Balint15]: Bálint M et al. 2015. Relocation, high-latitude warming and host genetic identity shape the foliar fungal microbiome of poplars. Molecular Ecology 24, 235-248. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.13018
68	
69	```{r, fig.cap="Hill number 1"}
70	p <- MiscMetabar::hill_pq(data_fungi, fact = "Height")
71	p$plot_Hill_0
72	```
73	
74	```{r, fig.cap="Result of the Tuckey post-hoc test"}
75	p$plot_tuckey
76	```
77	
78	See also the [tutorial](https://microbiome.github.io/tutorials/Alphadiversity.html) of the microbiome package for an alternative using the non-parametric [Kolmogorov-Smirnov test](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/dgof/versions/1.2/topics/ks.test) for two-group comparisons when there are no relevant covariates.
79	
80	## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`

file.content:
---
title: "alpha-div"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{alpha-div}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{r setup, message=FALSE}
library(MiscMetabar)
data(data_fungi)
```

### Alpha diversity analysis

#### Hill number

Numerous metrics of diversity exist. 
Hill numbers [^Hill73] is a kind of general framework for alpha diversity index. 

[^Hill73]: Hill MO. 1973. Diversity and evenness: a unifying notation and its consequences. Ecology 54, 427-473.


#### Diversity profiles

`profile_hill_pq()` plots the Hill diversity profile (diversity as a function of the order *q*) for each sample, or for groups of samples merged via `merge_sample_by`.

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles per sample (data_fungi_mini)"}
data(data_fungi_mini)
dfm_rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi_mini, rngseed = 1, sample.size=200)
p <-profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) 

p + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height")
```

#### Rarefaction curves

`hill_acc_pq()` plots Hill diversity accumulation (rarefaction) curves — one per sample or per merged group — showing how estimated diversity grows with sequencing depth.

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves per sample (Hill order q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations=20) + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves merged by Height (Hill order q = 0)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations=20)
```

#### Test for difference in diversity (hill number)

One way to keep into account for difference in the number of sequences per samples is to use a Tukey test on a linear model with the square roots of the number of sequence as the first explanatory variable of the linear model [^Balint15].

[^Balint15]: Bálint M et al. 2015. Relocation, high-latitude warming and host genetic identity shape the foliar fungal microbiome of poplars. Molecular Ecology 24, 235-248. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.13018

```{r, fig.cap="Hill number 1"}
p <- MiscMetabar::hill_pq(data_fungi, fact = "Height")
p$plot_Hill_0
```

```{r, fig.cap="Result of the Tuckey post-hoc test"}
p$plot_tuckey
```

See also the [tutorial](https://microbiome.github.io/tutorials/Alphadiversity.html) of the microbiome package for an alternative using the non-parametric [Kolmogorov-Smirnov test](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/dgof/versions/1.2/topics/ks.test) for two-group comparisons when there are no relevant covariates.

## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`

80	## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`
81	
82	```{r}
83	library("MicrobiotaProcess")
84	clean_pq(subset_samples_pq(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))) %>%
85	  as.MPSE() %>%
86	  mp_cal_alpha() %>%
87	  mp_plot_alpha(.group = "Height")
88	```
89	
90	
91	## Estimation statistics framework
92	
93	" Estimation statistics is a simple framework that avoids the pitfalls of significance testing. It uses familiar statistical concepts: means, mean differences, and error bars. More importantly, it focuses on the effect size of one’s experiment/intervention, as opposed to a false dichotomy engendered by P values. " Citation from dabest documentation [website](https://acclab.github.io/dabestr/index.html).
94	
95	### Durga package
96	
97	```{r}
98	library("Durga")
99	psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)
100	
101	durga_res <- DurgaDiff(Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, psm)
102	DurgaPlot(durga_res)
103	```
104	
105	```{r}
106	durga_pq <- function(physeq, formula, plot = FALSE) {
107	  verify_pq(physeq)
108	  psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(physeq)
109	  res_durga <- DurgaDiff(formula, psm)
110	  if (plot) {
111	    p <- DurgaPlot(res_durga)
112	    invisible(p)
113	  } else {
114	    return(res_durga)
115	  }
116	}
117	
118	durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Height, plot = TRUE)
119	durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time + Height, plot = TRUE)
120	
121	durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
122	durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_1 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
123	durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_2 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
124	```
125	
126	
127	### dabest R package
128	
129	```{r}
130	library("dabestr")
131	psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)
132	
133	load(
134	  data = psm,
135	  x = Height,
136	  y = Hill_2,
137	  idx = list(c("Low", "Middle", "High"))
138	) %>%
139	  mean_diff() |>
140	  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_2")
141	```
142	
143	```{r}
144	psm |>
145	  mutate(Time_is_0 = Time == 0) |>
146	  load(
147	    x = Time_is_0,
148	    y = Hill_0,
149	    idx = list(c("TRUE", "FALSE"))
150	  ) %>%
151	  mean_diff() |>
152	  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_0")
153	```
154	
155	## Effect of samples variables on alpha diversity using automated model selection and multimodel inference with (G)LMs 
156	
157	From the help of glmulti package : 
158	
159	> glmulti finds what are the n best models (the confidence set of models) among all possible models (the candidate set, as specified by the user). Models are fitted with the specified fitting function (default is glm) and are ranked with the specified Information Criterion (default is aicc). The best models are found either through exhaustive screening of the candidates, or using a genetic algorithm, which allows very large candidate sets to be addressed. The output can be used for model selection, variable selection, and multimodel inference.
160	
161	```{r}
162	library("glmulti")
163	formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Hill_1 + Abundance + Time + Height"
164	res_glmulti <-
165	  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 1)
166	res_glmulti
167	
168	ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
169	  geom_point(
170	    size = 2,
171	    alpha = 1,
172	    show.legend = FALSE
173	  ) +
174	  geom_vline(
175	    xintercept = 0,
176	    linetype = "dotted",
177	    linewidth = 1
178	  ) +
179	  geom_errorbar(
180	    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
181	    width = 0.8,
182	    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
183	    alpha = 0.7,
184	    show.legend = FALSE
185	  ) +
186	  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
187	  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
188	  ylab(formula)
189	
190	ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(
191	  x = importance,
192	  y = as.factor(variable),
193	  fill = estimates
194	)) +
195	  geom_bar(
196	    stat = "identity",
197	    show.legend = FALSE,
198	    alpha = 0.8
199	  ) +
200	  xlim(c(0, 1)) +
201	  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model, x = 0.1),
202	    size = 3,
203	    fill = "white"
204	  ) +
205	  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
206	  xlab("Importance") +
207	  ylab(formula)
208	```

## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`

```{r}
library("MicrobiotaProcess")
clean_pq(subset_samples_pq(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))) %>%
  as.MPSE() %>%
  mp_cal_alpha() %>%
  mp_plot_alpha(.group = "Height")
```


## Estimation statistics framework

" Estimation statistics is a simple framework that avoids the pitfalls of significance testing. It uses familiar statistical concepts: means, mean differences, and error bars. More importantly, it focuses on the effect size of one’s experiment/intervention, as opposed to a false dichotomy engendered by P values. " Citation from dabest documentation [website](https://acclab.github.io/dabestr/index.html).

### Durga package

```{r}
library("Durga")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

durga_res <- DurgaDiff(Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, psm)
DurgaPlot(durga_res)
```

```{r}
durga_pq <- function(physeq, formula, plot = FALSE) {
  verify_pq(physeq)
  psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(physeq)
  res_durga <- DurgaDiff(formula, psm)
  if (plot) {
    p <- DurgaPlot(res_durga)
    invisible(p)
  } else {
    return(res_durga)
  }
}

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Height, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time + Height, plot = TRUE)

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_1 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_2 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
```


### dabest R package

```{r}
library("dabestr")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

load(
  data = psm,
  x = Height,
  y = Hill_2,
  idx = list(c("Low", "Middle", "High"))
) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_2")
```

```{r}
psm |>
  mutate(Time_is_0 = Time == 0) |>
  load(
    x = Time_is_0,
    y = Hill_0,
    idx = list(c("TRUE", "FALSE"))
  ) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_0")
```

## Effect of samples variables on alpha diversity using automated model selection and multimodel inference with (G)LMs 

From the help of glmulti package : 

> glmulti finds what are the n best models (the confidence set of models) among all possible models (the candidate set, as specified by the user). Models are fitted with the specified fitting function (default is glm) and are ranked with the specified Information Criterion (default is aicc). The best models are found either through exhaustive screening of the candidates, or using a genetic algorithm, which allows very large candidate sets to be addressed. The output can be used for model selection, variable selection, and multimodel inference.

```{r}
library("glmulti")
formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Hill_1 + Abundance + Time + Height"
res_glmulti <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 1)
res_glmulti

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
    width = 0.8,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
  ylab(formula)

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(
  x = importance,
  y = as.factor(variable),
  fill = estimates
)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    show.legend = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  xlim(c(0, 1)) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model, x = 0.1),
    size = 3,
    fill = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  xlab("Importance") +
  ylab(formula)
```


```{r}
formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Abundance + Time + Height"
res_glmulti_interaction <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 2)
res_glmulti_interaction

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
    width = 0.8,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
  ylab(formula)

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(

### Using Deseq2 package

```{r}
data("GlobalPatterns", package = "phyloseq")
GP <- subset_samples(
  GlobalPatterns,
  GlobalPatterns@sam_data$SampleType %in% c("Soil", "Skin")
)

plot_deseq2_pq(GP, c("SampleType", "Soil", "Skin"), pval = 0.001)
```


### Using Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) Effect Size (LEfSe)

```{r}
res_lefse <- lefser_pq(data_fungi, bifactor = "Height", modalities = c("Low", "High"))
lefser::lefserPlot(res_lefse)

res_lefse_clade <- lefser_pq(data_fungi, bifactor = "Height", modalities = c("Low", "High"), by_clade = TRUE)
lefser::lefserPlot(res_lefse_clade)
```

### Using ALDEx2 package

```{r}
res_aldex <- aldex_pq(data_fungi_mini, bifactor = "Height", modalities = c("Low", "High"))

ALDEx2::aldex.plot(res_aldex, type = "volcano")
```



### Using ancombc

```{r}
res_ancombc <- ancombc_pq(
  data_fungi_mini,
  fact = "Height",
  levels_fact = c("Low", "High"),
  verbose = TRUE
)
```

1	---
2	title: "Examples with published dataset: Tengeler"
3	output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
4	vignette: >
5	  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Examples with published dataset: Tengeler}
6	  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
7	  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
8	---
9	
10	This tutorial explore a phyloseq version of the dataset from Tengeler et al. (2020) available in the `mia` package.
11	
12	```{r, include = FALSE}
13	knitr::opts_chunk$set(
14	  collapse = TRUE,
15	  comment = "#>",
16	  message = FALSE,
17	  fig.width = 10
18	)
19	```
20	
21	# Load library 
22	
23	```{r}
24	library("MicrobiotaProcess")
25	library("MiscMetabar")
26	library("ggplot2")
27	library("patchwork")
28	library("iNEXT")
29	```
30	
31	
32	```{r, eval=FALSE}
33	?Tengeler2020
34	```
35

37	# Import dataset in phyloseq format
38	
39	
40	```{r}
41	data(Tengeler2020_pq)
42	ten <- Tengeler2020_pq
43	summary_plot_pq(ten)
44	```

50	```

54	  iNEXT_pq(ten,
55	    datatype = "abundance",
56	    merge_sample_by = "patient_status_vs_cohort",
57	    nboot = 5

59	ggiNEXT(res_inext)
60	```

64	  ten,
65	  fact = "sample_name",
66	  add_nb_seq = TRUE,

file.content:
---
title: "Examples with published dataset: Tengeler"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Examples with published dataset: Tengeler}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

This tutorial explore a phyloseq version of the dataset from Tengeler et al. (2020) available in the `mia` package.

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width = 10
)
```

# Load library 

```{r}
library("MicrobiotaProcess")
library("MiscMetabar")
library("ggplot2")
library("patchwork")
library("iNEXT")
```


```{r, eval=FALSE}
?Tengeler2020
```


# Import dataset in phyloseq format


```{r}
data(Tengeler2020_pq)
ten <- Tengeler2020_pq
summary_plot_pq(ten)
```

# Alpha-diversity analysis

```{r}
hill_pq(ten, "patient_status", one_plot = TRUE)
```

```{r}
res_inext <-
  iNEXT_pq(ten,
    datatype = "abundance",
    merge_sample_by = "patient_status_vs_cohort",
    nboot = 5
  )
ggiNEXT(res_inext)
```

```{r}
accu_plot(
  ten,
  fact = "sample_name",
  add_nb_seq = TRUE,
  by.fact = TRUE,
  step = 100
) + theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.6))
```

# Explore taxonomy

```{r}
# library(metacoder)
# heat_tree_pq(
#   ten,
#   node_size = n_obs,
#   node_color = nb_sequences,
#   node_label = taxon_names,
#   tree_label = taxon_names,
#   node_size_trans = "log10 area"
# )
```

```{r}
treemap_pq(ten, lvl1 = "Order", lvl2 = "Family")
```

# Beta-diversity analysis : effect of patient status and cohort

```{r}
circle_pq(ten, "patient_status")
```

```{r}
upset_pq(ten, "patient_status_vs_cohort")
```

```{r}
ggvenn_pq(clean_pq(ten, force_taxa_as_columns = TRUE),
  "cohort",
  rarefy_before_merging = TRUE
) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```


```{r}
ten_control <- clean_pq(subset_samples(ten, patient_status == "Control"))
# p_control <- heat_tree_pq(
#   ten_control,
#   node_size = n_obs,
#   node_color = nb_sequences,
#   node_label = taxon_names,
#   tree_label = taxon_names,
#   node_size_trans = "log10 area"
# )

ten_ADHD <- clean_pq(subset_samples(ten, patient_status == "ADHD"))
# p_ADHD <- heat_tree_pq(
#   ten_ADHD,
#   node_size = n_obs,
#   node_color = nb_sequences,
#   node_label = taxon_names,
#   tree_label = taxon_names,
#   node_size_trans = "log10 area"
# )
#
# p_control + ggtitle("Control") + p_ADHD + ggtitle("ADHD")
```


```{r}
knitr::kable(track_wkflow(list(
  "All samples" = ten,
  "Control samples" = ten_control,
  "ADHD samples" = ten_ADHD
)))
```

```{r}
adonis_pq(ten, "cohort + patient_status")
```


```{r}
ten@tax_table <- phyloseq::tax_table(cbind(
  ten@tax_table,
  "Species" = taxa_names(ten)
))

biplot_pq(subset_taxa_pq(ten, taxa_sums(ten) > 3000),
  merge_sample_by = "patient_status",
  fact = "patient_status",
  nudge_y = 0.4
)
```

The x-axis represents the 74 #' number of samples. 75 #' 76 #' @inheritParams clean_pq 77 #' @param q (numeric, default 1) Hill diversity order. Either `"individual"` 82 #' (sequence-based, one curve per sample) or `"sample"` (sample-based, 83 #' one curve for the whole dataset or per group). 84 #' @param merge_sample_by (character or NULL) Variable name in `sam_data` used 85 #' to group samples. g) 165 accum <- .hill_acc_sample( 166 otu[idx, , drop = FALSE], 167 q = q, 168 n_permutations = n_permutations, 169 conf_level = conf_level, 170 ..., 171 .return_df = TRUE 172 ) 173 accum$group <- g content.text:
Reviewing your trajectory: you've surveyed, identified concrete optimization candidates, and are about to dive into Tier 4 function-body changes. Three flags before you commit:

**1. Order is backward for the stated goal.** The user said the focus is "speed up use of the package and the pkgdown building." Tier 3 (article `n_permutations` reductions) is the most direct hit for pkgdown — zero risk, no internal code change, exactly what was asked for. Do it before touching function bodies. Right now you have it scheduled after the riskier work.

**2. You're about to optimize from code reading, not measurement.** Your hypothesis: `.hill_acc_sample` is quadratic in samples (cumulative-sum win), `verify_tax_table` has slow nested loops (vectorize win). But the 549s baseline killer was `hill_acc_pq` in `"individual"` mode — which goes through `divent::accum_hill`, not `.hill_acc_sample`. Sample mode may already be cheap. Verify with `bench::mark` on a 45×1420 OTU table before refactoring `.hill_acc_sample`. Same for `verify_tax_table`: confirm it's the inner loop and not e.g. `as.matrix(physeq@tax_table)` or one of the regex passes that dominates.

**3. CLAUDE.md says "When unsure about implementation details, ALWAYS ask the developer" and "prefer the boring solution or ask for help."** Tier 4 has two parts: "document timing" (documentation, explicit user request, low risk) and "try to speed up slow functions" (hedged "try to", touches function internals, JOSS-published reproducibility surface). Before refactoring `.hill_acc_sample` and `verify_tax_table` internals, ask the developer: "Should Tier 4 attempt internal optimization, or document-only? If optimize, which functions are priority?" The cumulative-sum rewrite is mathematically equivalent but changes floating-point summation order — `quantile()` outputs on the envelope could shift in low-significance digits. For a published package, that's worth confirming before doing.

**Suggested reordering:**
1. Update plan file (already in progress — fine)
2. Tier 3 article edits (#9) — cheapest, biggest pkgdown win, do now
3. Timing vignette (#12) — explicit user request, low risk; running it will also serve as profiling
4. Ask developer: "want me to attempt function-internal speedups, and if so which?"
5. Only then (#10, #11)

The timing vignette you're going to write *is* the profiling for step 4 — write it on the real datasets, let the numbers tell you and the developer where the cost lives, then decide whether internal changes are worth the risk.

The advisor flagged that I should reorder: (a) do Tier 3 first since it's the safest, biggest pkgdown win, (b) use the timing vignette itself as the profiling step, and (c) confirm with you before touching function internals (JOSS-published, reproducibility surface).

input.content:
# Speed up build / test / example / pkgdown in MiscMetabar

## Status: Tier 1/2 applied — Tier 3/4 in progress

Branch: `dev`. Tier 1 (examples) and Tier 2 (tests) landed and are
verified by repeated `devtools::check(args = c("--timings", "--run-donttest"))`.

| Measure | Baseline | After Tier 1/2 |
|---|---|---|
| Examples total elapsed | **1180 s / 19.7 min** | **447 s / 7.45 min** |
| Examples > 5 s | 25 | 17 (most are vsearch/mmseqs2/lulu — skipped on CRAN) |
| Slowest single example | `hill_acc_pq` 549 s | `verify_tax_table` 30 s (CRAN-relevant) |
| `R CMD check --as-cran --run-donttest` | over 10-min budget → rejected | examples pass; tests pass on dev |

Tier 3 (pkgdown articles) and Tier 4 (timing vignette + function-internal
speedups) are now active. Scope below.

## Context

CRAN rejected `MiscMetabar 0.16.5.9000` because `R CMD check --as-cran` exceeded the 10-minute budget. The May 2025 fix moved `verify_tax_table()`'s heavy call into `\donttest{}` but CRAN runs `--run-donttest`, so the wrapping did nothing. Static analysis missed `hill_acc_pq` — which turned out to be the dominant cost (549 s alone, ~46 % of total example time).

Two facts that shaped the work:

1. `vignettes/articles/` is in `.Rbuildignore`. Only `vignettes/MiscMetabar.Rmd` (2.9 kB) is built by `R CMD check`, so article speedups help **pkgdown CI only**, not CRAN. Tier 3 is now active because pkgdown speed is the new focus.
2. `\donttest{}` is executed under `--run-donttest`. `\dontrun{}` is not. Heavy examples that can't be shrunk go to `\dontrun{}`.

User-chosen scope: examples + tests only for Tier 1/2, preserve scientific defaults (`nperm`, `n_permutations`), no CI changes. For Tier 3/4 the scope is package-usage speed and pkgdown-build speed, with function-internal changes only after measurement and developer confirmation.

## What was done (Tier 1 / Tier 2)

### Tier 1 — example fixes (CRAN-blocker)

**Swapped to `data_fungi_mini` (137 × 45 vs. data_fungi's 185 × 1420):**

| File | Function(s) |
|---|---|
| `R/dada_phyloseq.R` | `verify_tax_table` (82 s → 30 s), `verify_pq` (82 s → < 5 s), `track_wkflow`, `reorder_taxa_pq`, `taxa_only_in_one_level` |
| `R/beta_div_test.R` | `adonis_pq`, `plot_SCBD_pq`, `multipatt_pq`, `adonis_rarperm_pq`, `lefser_pq`, `var_par_pq`, `var_par_rarperm_pq`, `distri_1_taxa` (also ASV2 → ASV7 since mini lacks ASV2) |
| `R/plot_functions.R` | `hill_pq`, `summary_plot_pq`, `ggvenn_pq`, `plot_tsne_pq`, `upset_test_pq`, `accu_plot_balanced_modality`, `multi_biplot_pq`, `tax_bar_pq`, `plot_refseq_pq`, `plot_seq_ratio_pq`, `plot_refseq_extremity_pq`, `plot_var_part_pq` |
| `R/alpha_div_test.R` | `glmutli_pq` |
| `R/normalize_pq.R` | `transform_pq` 16-method demo |

**Wrapped in `\dontrun{}` (function inherently slow even on subsets):**

| File:line | Function | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `R/hill_divent.R:112` | `hill_acc_pq` | Individual-mode accumulation has O(n²) cost; ~50 s even on mini. Documented in `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd`. |
| `R/plot_functions.R:3898` | `iNEXT_pq` | `iNEXT::iNEXT` internal interpolation; 86 s even with `nboot = 2`. |
| `R/taxonomy.R:498` | `format2dada2` | Fasta reformatting of `mini_UNITE_fungi.fasta.gz`; 25 s. |
| `R/alpha_div_test.R:198` | `hill_test_rarperm_pq` | Permutation × rarefaction × q-loop; 47 s on `data_fungi` even with `nperm = 3`, and `data_fungi_mini` was too sparse (PMCMR `data.frame` row mismatch). |

**Kept on `data_fungi` (mini was too sparse):**

- `R/plot_functions.R` `ggbetween_pq` — reverted to `data_fungi`; mini caused `rarefy_even_depth: sample.size less than or equal to zero`. 21 s, acceptable.

### Tier 2 — test fixes (local devtools::test() speed)

- `tests/testthat/test_figures_beta_div.R`: 8 × `nperm = 100` → `nperm = 9` (tests only assert return class).

### Docs & NEWS

- `man/*.Rd`: 33 files regenerated via `devtools::document()`.
- `NEWS.md`: development-version entry documenting all of the above.

### Function defaults

**Untouched.** `adonis_rarperm_pq` `nperm = 99`, `multipatt_pq` `control = permute::how(nperm = 999)`, `hill_acc_pq` `n_permutations = 100`, etc. all remain at their published values per user choice (reproducibility-sensitive, JOSS-published).

## Tier 3 — pkgdown article speedups (active)

Heavy chunks (located by `grep -nE "n_permutations|n_simulations|aldex|ancombc|iNEXT|hill_acc"` over `vignettes/articles/*.Rmd`):

| File | Line(s) | Current | New |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 252 | `n_simulations = 10` (individual rarefaction, full metacommunity) | `n_simulations = 0` (envelope-free, just the curve) |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 320 | `n_permutations = 50` (sample-based, full dataset) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 334 | `n_permutations = 50` (sample-based, by Height) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 361 | `n_permutations = 50` (incidence-based, full) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 378 | `n_permutations = 50` (incidence-based, by Height) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 405, 418 | `profile_hill_pq(..., n_simulations = 10)` | `n_simulations = 0` (no envelope) |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 56 | `hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations=20)` | `n_simulations = 0` |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 60 | `hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, ..., n_simulations=20)` | `n_simulations = 0` |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 70, 99, 118-123, 131, 165, 213 | `data_fungi` for `hill_pq`, `psmelt_samples_pq`, `glmutli_pq`, etc. | `data_fungi_mini` where the demo still teaches the function |
| `vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd` | 32 | `adonis_pq(data_fungi_woNA4height, "Tree_name+Height")` | `adonis_pq(data_fungi_mini, "Height")` (mini has fewer factors) — confirm chunk text still reads |
| `vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd` | 216, 219 | `lefser_pq(data_fungi, …)` | already mini if subset; else add `data_fungi_mini` |
| `vignettes/articles/tengeler.Rmd` | 53-58 | `iNEXT_pq(ten, …, nboot = 5)` | keep `nboot = 5` but ensure `merge_sample_by` reduces matrix; verify acceptable |

The rule for each edit: keep one explicit demonstration call per function so users still see what it does, just on a smaller input or with fewer iterations. Confidence envelopes (`n_simulations`, `n_permutations`) are illustrative in articles, not statistically demonstrative; users who need real numbers will read the function docs.

## Tier 4 — timing vignette + function-internal speedups (active)

### 4a — Timing vignette (always applicable)

New article `vignettes/articles/timing.Rmd` documenting wall-clock cost of
the public functions on `data_fungi` (185 × 1420) and `data_fungi_mini`
(137 × 45). One table per family (alpha, beta, ordination, taxonomy,
filtering, plotting) with rows = function, cols = dataset, cells =
elapsed time from `microbenchmark::microbenchmark(times = 3)` (or
`bench::mark(iterations = 3)`).

Output of the chunk is cached (`cache = TRUE`) so the article re-renders
cheaply on pkgdown unless the underlying code changed.

### 4b — Function-internal speedups (only with developer confirmation)

Candidates identified by static analysis, to be confirmed by the timing
vignette and then by the developer before touching:

| Function | Current cost | Hypothesised fix |
|---|---|---|
| `.hill_acc_sample` (in `R/hill_divent.R`) | O(n² · taxa · permutations): `colSums(otu_mat[perm[seq_len(k)], , drop = FALSE])` recomputed from scratch for every k | Replace inner loop with cumulative sum (`apply(otu_mat[perm, ], 2, cumsum)`), keeping the same `divent::div_hill` call per step. Math is equivalent, floating-point summation order changes. Quantile envelope would be sensitive in low digits — needs developer sign-off. |
| `verify_tax_table` | Nested `for (j in ranks) for (i in nrow)` over a 1420×7 character matrix, ~10 passes (NA-like, short, whitespace, invisible chars, redundant suffix) | Vectorise each pass: use `grepl(..., perl = TRUE)` over the whole matrix (returns a logical matrix), `which(..., arr.ind = TRUE)` to find positions. ~10× speedup expected without changing semantics. |
| `iNEXT_pq` | Wraps `iNEXT::iNEXT`. We don't own the inner loop. | Out of scope unless we can pre-subset the input matrix (e.g. drop singletons before passing to `iNEXT`). |
| `format2dada2` | Fasta read + per-record string manipulation | Check if `seqinr::read.fasta` + vectorised regex would replace the per-record `for` loop. |
| `hill_test_rarperm_pq` | Permutation × rarefaction × q-loop, three nested loops, PMCMR post-hoc per permutation | Investigate whether the rarefaction can be precomputed once per permutation and reused across q values. |
| `adonis_rarperm_pq`, `var_par_rarperm_pq`, etc. | Rarefaction × permutation loop in pure R | Same idea: factor out the rarefaction so it's only done once per permutation. |

### Critical files for Tier 3 / Tier 4

- `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd`, `alpha-div.Rmd`, `beta-div.Rmd`, `tengeler.Rmd` (Tier 3)
- `vignettes/articles/timing.Rmd` (new, Tier 4a)
- `R/hill_divent.R`, `R/dada_phyloseq.R` (Tier 4b — only with confirmation)
- `_pkgdown.yml` (add the new article to the navbar)
- `NEWS.md` (add entries)

## What was deliberately not done

- **No CI changes** (per user choice). `.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml` still uses `--no-examples --no-tests --no-vignettes`; the maintainer must run `--as-cran --run-donttest` locally before each submission. A future round should add a CRAN-realistic CI job.

## Remaining NOTEs (tracked separately, not blocking)

- `Namespace in Imports field not imported from: 'XVector'` — already removed in `dev`.
- `Depends: includes too many packages` — `0.17.0` migration to `Imports` is queued (see memory).

## Verification

Reproduce baseline / after with:

```r
devtools::document()                                       # regen Rd
devtools::check(args = c("--timings", "--run-donttest"))   # the only check that matches CRAN
# Read MiscMetabar.Rcheck/MiscMetabar-Ex.timings and look at the elapsed column.
```

For Tier 3 / Tier 4:

```r
# Time a single article render before and after edits
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd"))
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd"))
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd"))

# End-to-end pkgdown
system.time(pkgdown::build_articles(lazy = FALSE))
```

Acceptance check:
- ✅ Total examples elapsed < 600 s (got 447 s).
- ✅ No single CRAN-relevant example > 60 s (max is `verify_tax_table` 30 s).
- ✅ `tests/testthat/test_figures_beta_div.R` passes cleanly under `NOT_CRAN=true`.
- ⏳ Tier 3 target: each of the four heavy articles renders in < 60 s on the dev laptop (baseline 3-7 min each).
- ⏳ Tier 4 target: timing vignette published; `verify_tax_table(data_fungi)` < 5 s; `.hill_acc_sample` benchmark documented in the vignette before/after any internal change.

Ready for resubmission already (Tier 1/2 done). Tier 3/4 is for pkgdown speed and user-facing function speed; not blocking CRAN.

| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 320 | `n_permutations = 50` (sample-based, full dataset) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 334 | `n_permutations = 50` (sample-based, by Height) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 361 | `n_permutations = 50` (incidence-based, full) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 378 | `n_permutations = 50` (incidence-based, by Height) | `n_permutations = 10` |
| `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd` | 405, 418 | `profile_hill_pq(..., n_simulations = 10)` | `n_simulations = 0` (no envelope) |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 56 | `hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations=20)` | `n_simulations = 0` |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 60 | `hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, ..., n_simulations=20)` | `n_simulations = 0` |
| `vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd` | 70, 99, 118-123, 131, 165, 213 | `data_fungi` for `hill_pq`, `psmelt_samples_pq`, `glmutli_pq`, etc. | `data_fungi_mini` where the demo still teaches the function |
| `vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd` | 32 | `adonis_pq(data_fungi_woNA4height, "Tree_name+Height")` | `adonis_pq(data_fungi_mini, "Height")` (mini has fewer factors) — confirm chunk text still reads |
| `vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd` | 216, 219 | `lefser_pq(data_fungi, …)` | already mini if subset; else add `data_fungi_mini` |
| `vignettes/articles/tengeler.Rmd` | 53-58 | `iNEXT_pq(ten, …, nboot = 5)` | keep `nboot = 5` but ensure `merge_sample_by` reduces matrix; verify acceptable |

The rule for each edit: keep one explicit demonstration call per function so users still see what it does, just on a smaller input or with fewer iterations. Confidence envelopes (`n_simulations`, `n_permutations`) are illustrative in articles, not statistically demonstrative; users who need real numbers will read the function docs.

## Tier 4 — timing vignette + function-internal speedups (active)

### 4a — Timing vignette (always applicable)

New article `vignettes/articles/timing.Rmd` documenting wall-clock cost of
the public functions on `data_fungi` (185 × 1420) and `data_fungi_mini`
(137 × 45). One table per family (alpha, beta, ordination, taxonomy,
filtering, plotting) with rows = function, cols = dataset, cells =
elapsed time from `microbenchmark::microbenchmark(times = 3)` (or
`bench::mark(iterations = 3)`).

Output of the chunk is cached (`cache = TRUE`) so the article re-renders
cheaply on pkgdown unless the underlying code changed.

### 4b — Function-internal speedups (only with developer confirmation)

Candidates identified by static analysis, to be confirmed by the timing
vignette and then by the developer before touching:

| Function | Current cost | Hypothesised fix |
|---|---|---|
| `.hill_acc_sample` (in `R/hill_divent.R`) | O(n² · taxa · permutations): `colSums(otu_mat[perm[seq_len(k)], , drop = FALSE])` recomputed from scratch for every k | Replace inner loop with cumulative sum (`apply(otu_mat[perm, ], 2, cumsum)`), keeping the same `divent::div_hill` call per step. Math is equivalent, floating-point summation order changes. Quantile envelope would be sensitive in low digits — needs developer sign-off. |
| `verify_tax_table` | Nested `for (j in ranks) for (i in nrow)` over a 1420×7 character matrix, ~10 passes (NA-like, short, whitespace, invisible chars, redundant suffix) | Vectorise each pass: use `grepl(..., perl = TRUE)` over the whole matrix (returns a logical matrix), `which(..., arr.ind = TRUE)` to find positions. ~10× speedup expected without changing semantics. |
| `iNEXT_pq` | Wraps `iNEXT::iNEXT`. We don't own the inner loop. | Out of scope unless we can pre-subset the input matrix (e.g. drop singletons before passing to `iNEXT`). |
| `format2dada2` | Fasta read + per-record string manipulation | Check if `seqinr::read.fasta` + vectorised regex would replace the per-record `for` loop. |
| `hill_test_rarperm_pq` | Permutation × rarefaction × q-loop, three nested loops, PMCMR post-hoc per permutation | Investigate whether the rarefaction can be precomputed once per permutation and reused across q values. |
| `adonis_rarperm_pq`, `var_par_rarperm_pq`, etc. | Rarefaction × permutation loop in pure R | Same idea: factor out the rarefaction so it's only done once per permutation. |

### Critical files for Tier 3 / Tier 4

- `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd`, `alpha-div.Rmd`, `beta-div.Rmd`, `tengeler.Rmd` (Tier 3)
- `vignettes/articles/timing.Rmd` (new, Tier 4a)
- `R/hill_divent.R`, `R/dada_phyloseq.R` (Tier 4b — only with confirmation)
- `_pkgdown.yml` (add the new article to the navbar)
- `NEWS.md` (add entries)

## What was deliberately not done

- **No CI changes** (per user choice). `.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml` still uses `--no-examples --no-tests --no-vignettes`; the maintainer must run `--as-cran --run-donttest` locally before each submission. A future round should add a CRAN-realistic CI job.

## Remaining NOTEs (tracked separately, not blocking)

- `Namespace in Imports field not imported from: 'XVector'` — already removed in `dev`.
- `Depends: includes too many packages` — `0.17.0` migration to `Imports` is queued (see memory).

## Verification

Reproduce baseline / after with:

```r
devtools::document()                                       # regen Rd
devtools::check(args = c("--timings", "--run-donttest"))   # the only check that matches CRAN
# Read MiscMetabar.Rcheck/MiscMetabar-Ex.timings and look at the elapsed column.
```

For Tier 3 / Tier 4:

```r
# Time a single article render before and after edits
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd"))
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/alpha-div.Rmd"))
system.time(rmarkdown::render("vignettes/articles/beta-div.Rmd"))

# End-to-end pkgdown
system.time(pkgdown::build_articles(lazy = FALSE))
```

Acceptance check:
- ✅ Total examples elapsed < 600 s (got 447 s).
- ✅ No single CRAN-relevant example > 60 s (max is `verify_tax_table` 30 s).
- ✅ `tests/testthat/test_figures_beta_div.R` passes cleanly under `NOT_CRAN=true`.
- ⏳ Tier 3 target: each of the four heavy articles renders in < 60 s on the dev laptop (baseline 3-7 min each).
- ⏳ Tier 4 target: timing vignette published; `verify_tax_table(data_fungi)` < 5 s; `.hill_acc_sample` benchmark documented in the vignette before/after any internal change.

Ready for resubmission already (Tier 1/2 done). Tier 3/4 is for pkgdown speed and user-facing function speed; not blocking CRAN.

# Speed up build / test / example / pkgdown in MiscMetabar

## Status: applied — examples now fit CRAN budget

Branch: `dev`. All edits implemented and verified by repeated `devtools::check(args = c("--timings", "--run-donttest"))`.

| Measure | Baseline | After |
|---|---|---|
| Examples total elapsed | **1180 s / 19.7 min** | **447 s / 7.45 min** |
| Examples > 5 s | 25 | 17 (most are vsearch/mmseqs2/lulu — skipped on CRAN) |
| Slowest single example | `hill_acc_pq` 549 s | `verify_tax_table` 30 s (CRAN-relevant) |
| `R CMD check --as-cran --run-donttest` status | over 10-min budget → rejected | examples pass; tests pass on the dev branch |

## Context

CRAN rejected `MiscMetabar 0.16.5.9000` because `R CMD check --as-cran` exceeded the 10-minute budget. The May 2025 fix moved `verify_tax_table()`'s heavy call into `\donttest{}` but CRAN runs `--run-donttest`, so the wrapping did nothing. Static analysis missed `hill_acc_pq` — which turned out to be the dominant cost (549 s alone, ~46 % of total example time).

Two facts that shaped the work:

1. `vignettes/articles/` is in `.Rbuildignore`. Only `vignettes/MiscMetabar.Rmd` (2.9 kB) is built by `R CMD check`, so article speedups help **pkgdown CI only**, not CRAN. Tier 3 was deferred.
2. `\donttest{}` is executed under `--run-donttest`. `\dontrun{}` is not. The user-facing cost of switching is small (CRAN's example output isn't where users discover the function), so heavy examples that can't be shrunk go to `\dontrun{}`.

User-chosen scope: examples + tests only, preserve scientific defaults (`nperm`, `n_permutations`), no CI changes this round.

## What was done

### Tier 1 — example fixes (CRAN-blocker)

**Swapped to `data_fungi_mini` (137 × 45 vs. data_fungi's 185 × 1420):**

| File | Function(s) |
|---|---|
| `R/dada_phyloseq.R` | `verify_tax_table` (82 s → 30 s), `verify_pq` (82 s → < 5 s), `track_wkflow`, `reorder_taxa_pq`, `taxa_only_in_one_level` |
| `R/beta_div_test.R` | `adonis_pq`, `plot_SCBD_pq`, `multipatt_pq`, `adonis_rarperm_pq`, `lefser_pq`, `var_par_pq`, `var_par_rarperm_pq`, `distri_1_taxa` (also ASV2 → ASV7 since mini lacks ASV2) |
| `R/plot_functions.R` | `hill_pq`, `summary_plot_pq`, `ggvenn_pq`, `plot_tsne_pq`, `upset_test_pq`, `accu_plot_balanced_modality`, `multi_biplot_pq`, `tax_bar_pq`, `plot_refseq_pq`, `plot_seq_ratio_pq`, `plot_refseq_extremity_pq`, `plot_var_part_pq` |
| `R/alpha_div_test.R` | `glmutli_pq` |
| `R/normalize_pq.R` | `transform_pq` 16-method demo |

**Wrapped in `\dontrun{}` (function inherently slow even on subsets):**

| File:line | Function | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `R/hill_divent.R:112` | `hill_acc_pq` | Individual-mode accumulation has O(n²) cost; ~50 s even on mini. Documented in `vignettes/articles/accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd`. |
| `R/plot_functions.R:3898` | `iNEXT_pq` | `iNEXT::iNEXT` internal interpolation; 86 s even with `nboot = 2`. |
| `R/taxonomy.R:498` | `format2dada2` | Fasta reformatting of `mini_UNITE_fungi.fasta.gz`; 25 s. |
| `R/alpha_div_test.R:198` | `hill_test_rarperm_pq` | Permutation × rarefaction × q-loop; 47 s on `data_fungi` even with `nperm = 3`, and `data_fungi_mini` was too sparse (PMCMR `data.frame` row mismatch). |

**Kept on `data_fungi` (mini was too sparse):**

- `R/plot_functions.R` `ggbetween_pq` — reverted to `data_fungi`; mini caused `rarefy_even_depth: sample.size less than or equal to zero`. 21 s, acceptable.

### Tier 2 — test fixes (local devtools::test() speed)

- `tests/testthat/test_figures_beta_div.R`: 8 × `nperm = 100` → `nperm = 9` (tests only assert return class).

### Docs & NEWS

- `man/*.Rd`: 33 files regenerated via `devtools::document()`.
- `NEWS.md`: development-version entry documenting all of the above.

### Function defaults

**Untouched.** `adonis_rarperm_pq` `nperm = 99`, `multipatt_pq` `control = permute::how(nperm = 999)`, `hill_acc_pq` `n_permutations = 100`, etc. all remain at their published values per user choice (reproducibility-sensitive, JOSS-published).

## What was deliberately not done

- **No CI changes** (per user choice). `.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml` still uses `--no-examples --no-tests --no-vignettes`; the maintainer must run `--as-cran --run-donttest` locally before each submission to catch regressions. A future round should add a CRAN-realistic CI job.
- **Tier 3 (article speedups in `vignettes/articles/`)** deferred. These are excluded by `.Rbuildignore` and don't affect CRAN. They will speed up the pkgdown CI by ~3-5 min when needed:
  - `accumulation-rarefaction.Rmd`: `n_permutations = 50` → `10` at lines 315, 322, 328-337, 356-364, 373-380
  - `alpha-div.Rmd:56, 60`: `n_simulations = 20` → `5`
  - `beta-div.Rmd:226, 236`: subset for `aldex_pq` / `ancombc_pq`
- **Tier 4** : document the time for main functions in a new vignettes added to the pkgdown website and try to speed up slow functions. 
 
## Remaining NOTEs (tracked separately, not blocking)

- `Namespace in Imports field not imported from: 'XVector'` — already removed in `dev`.
- `Depends: includes too many packages` — `0.17.0` migration to `Imports` is queued (see memory).

## Verification

Reproduce baseline / after with:

```r
devtools::document()                                       # regen Rd
devtools::check(args = c("--timings", "--run-donttest"))   # the only check that matches CRAN
# Read MiscMetabar.Rcheck/MiscMetabar-Ex.timings and look at the elapsed column.
```

Acceptance check:
- ✅ Total examples elapsed < 600 s (got 447 s).
- ✅ No single CRAN-relevant example > 60 s (max is `verify_tax_table` 30 s).
- ✅ `tests/testthat/test_figures_beta_div.R` passes cleanly under `NOT_CRAN=true` (all tests pass; previous `ancombc_pq` data sanity issue resolved).
- ⚠️ Local `devtools::test()` still ~15 min; CRAN runs of `testthat.R` will be much shorter because of `skip_on_cran()` guards.

Ready for resubmission once Tier 4 follow-ups (if desired) are done.

---
title: "alpha-div"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{alpha-div}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{r setup, message=FALSE}
library(MiscMetabar)
data(data_fungi)
```

### Alpha diversity analysis

#### Hill number

Numerous metrics of diversity exist. 
Hill numbers [^Hill73] is a kind of general framework for alpha diversity index. 

[^Hill73]: Hill MO. 1973. Diversity and evenness: a unifying notation and its consequences. Ecology 54, 427-473.


#### Diversity profiles

`profile_hill_pq()` plots the Hill diversity profile (diversity as a function of the order *q*) for each sample, or for groups of samples merged via `merge_sample_by`.

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles per sample (data_fungi_mini)"}
data(data_fungi_mini)
dfm_rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi_mini, rngseed = 1, sample.size=200)
p <-profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) 

p + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height")
```

#### Rarefaction curves

`hill_acc_pq()` plots Hill diversity accumulation (rarefaction) curves — one per sample or per merged group — showing how estimated diversity grows with sequencing depth.

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves per sample (Hill order q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations=20) + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves merged by Height (Hill order q = 0)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations=20)
```

#### Test for difference in diversity (hill number)

One way to keep into account for difference in the number of sequences per samples is to use a Tukey test on a linear model with the square roots of the number of sequence as the first explanatory variable of the linear model [^Balint15].

[^Balint15]: Bálint M et al. 2015. Relocation, high-latitude warming and host genetic identity shape the foliar fungal microbiome of poplars. Molecular Ecology 24, 235-248. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.13018

```{r, fig.cap="Hill number 1"}
p <- MiscMetabar::hill_pq(data_fungi, fact = "Height")
p$plot_Hill_0
```

```{r, fig.cap="Result of the Tuckey post-hoc test"}
p$plot_tuckey
```

See also the [tutorial](https://microbiome.github.io/tutorials/Alphadiversity.html) of the microbiome package for an alternative using the non-parametric [Kolmogorov-Smirnov test](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/dgof/versions/1.2/topics/ks.test) for two-group comparisons when there are no relevant covariates.

## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`

```{r}
library("MicrobiotaProcess")
clean_pq(subset_samples_pq(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))) %>%
  as.MPSE() %>%
  mp_cal_alpha() %>%
  mp_plot_alpha(.group = "Height")
```


## Estimation statistics framework

" Estimation statistics is a simple framework that avoids the pitfalls of significance testing. It uses familiar statistical concepts: means, mean differences, and error bars. More importantly, it focuses on the effect size of one’s experiment/intervention, as opposed to a false dichotomy engendered by P values. " Citation from dabest documentation [website](https://acclab.github.io/dabestr/index.html).

### Durga package

```{r}
library("Durga")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

durga_res <- DurgaDiff(Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, psm)
DurgaPlot(durga_res)
```

```{r}
durga_pq <- function(physeq, formula, plot = FALSE) {
  verify_pq(physeq)
  psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(physeq)
  res_durga <- DurgaDiff(formula, psm)
  if (plot) {
    p <- DurgaPlot(res_durga)
    invisible(p)
  } else {
    return(res_durga)
  }
}

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Height, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time + Height, plot = TRUE)

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_1 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_2 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
```


### dabest R package

```{r}
library("dabestr")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

load(
  data = psm,
  x = Height,
  y = Hill_2,
  idx = list(c("Low", "Middle", "High"))
) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_2")
```

```{r}
psm |>
  mutate(Time_is_0 = Time == 0) |>
  load(
    x = Time_is_0,
    y = Hill_0,
    idx = list(c("TRUE", "FALSE"))
  ) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_0")
```

## Effect of samples variables on alpha diversity using automated model selection and multimodel inference with (G)LMs 

From the help of glmulti package : 

> glmulti finds what are the n best models (the confidence set of models) among all possible models (the candidate set, as specified by the user). Models are fitted with the specified fitting function (default is glm) and are ranked with the specified Information Criterion (default is aicc). The best models are found either through exhaustive screening of the candidates, or using a genetic algorithm, which allows very large candidate sets to be addressed. The output can be used for model selection, variable selection, and multimodel inference.

```{r}
library("glmulti")
formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Hill_1 + Abundance + Time + Height"
res_glmulti <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 1)
res_glmulti

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
    width = 0.8,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
  ylab(formula)

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(
  x = importance,
  y = as.factor(variable),
  fill = estimates
)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    show.legend = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  xlim(c(0, 1)) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model, x = 0.1),
    size = 3,
    fill = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  xlab("Importance") +
  ylab(formula)
```


```{r}
formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Abundance + Time + Height"
res_glmulti_interaction <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 2)
res_glmulti_interaction

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
    width = 0.8,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
  ylab(formula)

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(
  x = importance,
  y = as.factor(variable),
  fill = estimates
)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    show.legend = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  xlim(c(0, 1)) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model, x = 0.1),
    size = 3,
    fill = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  xlab("Importance") +
  ylab(formula)
```

```

  ) +

---
title: "alpha-div"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{alpha-div}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{r setup, message=FALSE}
library(MiscMetabar)
data(data_fungi)
```

### Alpha diversity analysis

#### Hill number

Numerous metrics of diversity exist. 
Hill numbers [^Hill73] is a kind of general framework for alpha diversity index. 

[^Hill73]: Hill MO. 1973. Diversity and evenness: a unifying notation and its consequences. Ecology 54, 427-473.


#### Diversity profiles

`profile_hill_pq()` plots the Hill diversity profile (diversity as a function of the order *q*) for each sample, or for groups of samples merged via `merge_sample_by`.

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles per sample (data_fungi_mini)"}
data(data_fungi_mini)
dfm_rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi_mini, rngseed = 1, sample.size=200)
p <-profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied) 

p + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Hill diversity profiles merged by Height"}
profile_hill_pq(dfm_rarefied, merge_sample_by = "Height")
```

#### Rarefaction curves

`hill_acc_pq()` plots Hill diversity accumulation (rarefaction) curves — one per sample or per merged group — showing how estimated diversity grows with sequencing depth.

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves per sample (Hill order q = 1)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 1, n_simulations = 0) + no_legend()
```

```{r, fig.cap="Rarefaction curves merged by Height (Hill order q = 0)"}
hill_acc_pq(dfm_rarefied, q = 0, merge_sample_by = "Height", n_simulations = 0)
```

#### Test for difference in diversity (hill number)

One way to keep into account for difference in the number of sequences per samples is to use a Tukey test on a linear model with the square roots of the number of sequence as the first explanatory variable of the linear model [^Balint15].

[^Balint15]: Bálint M et al. 2015. Relocation, high-latitude warming and host genetic identity shape the foliar fungal microbiome of poplars. Molecular Ecology 24, 235-248. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.13018

```{r, fig.cap="Hill number 1"}
p <- MiscMetabar::hill_pq(data_fungi, fact = "Height")
p$plot_Hill_0
```

```{r, fig.cap="Result of the Tuckey post-hoc test"}
p$plot_tuckey
```

See also the [tutorial](https://microbiome.github.io/tutorials/Alphadiversity.html) of the microbiome package for an alternative using the non-parametric [Kolmogorov-Smirnov test](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/dgof/versions/1.2/topics/ks.test) for two-group comparisons when there are no relevant covariates.

## Alpha diversity using package `MicrobiotaProcess`

```{r}
library("MicrobiotaProcess")
clean_pq(subset_samples_pq(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))) %>%
  as.MPSE() %>%
  mp_cal_alpha() %>%
  mp_plot_alpha(.group = "Height")
```


## Estimation statistics framework

" Estimation statistics is a simple framework that avoids the pitfalls of significance testing. It uses familiar statistical concepts: means, mean differences, and error bars. More importantly, it focuses on the effect size of one’s experiment/intervention, as opposed to a false dichotomy engendered by P values. " Citation from dabest documentation [website](https://acclab.github.io/dabestr/index.html).

### Durga package

```{r}
library("Durga")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

durga_res <- DurgaDiff(Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, psm)
DurgaPlot(durga_res)
```

```{r}
durga_pq <- function(physeq, formula, plot = FALSE) {
  verify_pq(physeq)
  psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(physeq)
  res_durga <- DurgaDiff(formula, psm)
  if (plot) {
    p <- DurgaPlot(res_durga)
    invisible(p)
  } else {
    return(res_durga)
  }
}

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Height, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time + Height, plot = TRUE)

durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_0 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_1 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
durga_pq(data_fungi, Hill_2 ~ Time == 0, plot = TRUE)
```


### dabest R package

```{r}
library("dabestr")
psm <- psmelt_samples_pq(data_fungi)

load(
  data = psm,
  x = Height,
  y = Hill_2,
  idx = list(c("Low", "Middle", "High"))
) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_2")
```

```{r}
psm |>
  mutate(Time_is_0 = Time == 0) |>
  load(
    x = Time_is_0,
    y = Hill_0,
    idx = list(c("TRUE", "FALSE"))
  ) %>%
  mean_diff() |>
  dabest_plot(swarm_label = "Hill_0")
```

## Effect of samples variables on alpha diversity using automated model selection and multimodel inference with (G)LMs 

From the help of glmulti package : 

> glmulti finds what are the n best models (the confidence set of models) among all possible models (the candidate set, as specified by the user). Models are fitted with the specified fitting function (default is glm) and are ranked with the specified Information Criterion (default is aicc). The best models are found either through exhaustive screening of the candidates, or using a genetic algorithm, which allows very large candidate sets to be addressed. The output can be used for model selection, variable selection, and multimodel inference.

```{r}
library("glmulti")
formula <- "Hill_0 ~ Hill_1 + Abundance + Time + Height"
res_glmulti <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 1)
res_glmulti

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dotted",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(xmin = estimates - alpha, xmax = estimates + alpha),
    width = 0.8,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3) +
  xlab("Standardized estimates") +
  ylab(formula)

ggplot(data = res_glmulti, aes(
  x = importance,
  y = as.factor(variable),
  fill = estimates
)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    show.legend = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  xlim(c(0, 1)) +
  geom_label(aes(label = nb_model, x = 0.1),
    size = 3,
    fill = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  xlab("Importance") +
  ylab(formula)
```

res_glmulti_interaction <-
  glmutli_pq(data_fungi, formula = formula, level = 2)
res_glmulti_interaction

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(x = estimates, y = variable)) +
  geom_point(
    size = 2,
    alpha = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +

  ) +

ggplot(data = res_glmulti_interaction, aes(

```

file.content:
---
title: "beta-div"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{beta-div}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---


```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{r setup, message=FALSE}
library(MiscMetabar)
library(lefser)
library(ALDEx2)
data(data_fungi)
```

## Permanova

```{r}
data_fungi_woNA4height <- subset_samples(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))
res_ado <- adonis_pq(data_fungi_woNA4height, "Tree_name+Height")
knitr::kable(res_ado)
```


## Graph Test

```{r}
data_fungi_woNA4height <- subset_samples(data_fungi, !is.na(data_fungi@sam_data$Height))
graph_test_pq(data_fungi_woNA4height, "Height")
```

## Circle of ASVs

```{r}
circle_pq(data_fungi_woNA4height, "Height")
```


## Ordination

### PCoA

```{r}
plot_ordination_pq(data_fungi, method = "robust.aitchison", ordination_method = "PCoA", color = "Height")
plot_ordination(data_fungi,
  ordination =
    ordinate(data_fungi, method = "PCoA", distance = "bray"), color = "Height"
)
```

### NMDS

```{r}
plot_ordination_pq(data_fungi, method = "robust.aitchison", color = "Height") +
  plot_ordination_pq(data_fungi, method = "bray", color = "Height")
```


### TSNE

```{r}
plot_tsne_pq(data_fungi, fact = "Height")
```


### UMAP

```{r}
df_umap <- umap_pq(data_fungi)
ggplot(df_umap, aes(x = x_umap, y = y_umap, col = Height)) +
  geom_point(size = 2)
```


## Compare two (group of) samples

### Biplot


```{r}
data_fungi_low_high <- subset_samples(
  data_fungi,
  data_fungi@sam_data$Height %in%
    c("Low", "High")
)
data_fungi_low_high <- subset_taxa_pq(
  data_fungi_low_high,
  taxa_sums(data_fungi_low_high) > 5000

content.text: Before I start internal function rewrites (Tier 4b), I want to confirm with you. The advisor flagged that the internal speedups touch JOSS-published functions and floating-point summation order, so they need your sign-off.

questions.question: For Tier 4b (function-internal speedups), how aggressive should I be?

options.label: Optimise the safe wins only (Recommended)
options.description: `.hill_acc_sample` cumulative-sum (O(n²) → O(n)) and `verify_tax_table` vectorisation. Both keep the public API and visible output identical; only floating-point summation order changes in `.hill_acc_sample` (envelope quantiles shift in low-significance digits).

options.label: All candidates: also touch hill_test_rarperm_pq, adonis_rarperm_pq, format2dada2
options.description: Bigger overall gain, but `*_rarperm_pq` are reproducibility-sensitive (rarefaction × permutation × q-loop). Floating-point output may shift; existing tests should still pass.

options.label: Document-only — no internal changes
options.description: Just produce the timing vignette and stop. No risk to JOSS-published numerics. Tier 3 pkgdown speedups stay.

questions.question: Should I add a new pkgdown article entry to the navbar for the timing vignette?

options.label: Yes, under "For developers" section (Recommended)
options.description: Add a `Function timings` entry next to `Rules of code` and `Report pkgnet`. Discoverable for users wondering about cost.

options.label: Yes, as a top-level article under Tutorials
options.description: Main `Articles` menu, alongside `Import/export and track` etc.

options.label: No, leave it accessible only via direct URL
options.description: Reduces visibility but keeps the navbar uncluttered.
